risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "They were randomly assigned to one of the three treatments: high dairy group, high calcium group, and control." Comment: The paper states that subjects were randomly assigned, but it does not describe the randomization method (e.g., computer generation, random numbers, blocks, stratification, or other mechanism). Under the requested criteria, "randomized" alone without context clues is insufficient to judge the sequence as truly random, so risk of bias for sequence generation is unclear.

Summary: The report states that subjects were "randomly assigned" to the treatment groups, but it does not describe any mechanism used to conceal allocation from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other method preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be judged and remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "single-center, randomized, double-blind (single-blinded for the dairy group), placebo-controlled trial" and "Subjects in the high elemental calcium group and subjects in the control group were instructed to maintain their usual diets." Comment: The study explicitly states that blinding was only single-blind for the dairy group, so participants/personnel were not fully blinded across all arms. Because the main outcomes were body composition and serum lipids, which could plausibly be influenced by knowing the assigned diet/supplement (e.g. diet adherence or co-interventions), the dominant risk for this domain is high.

Quote: "Measurements were taken ... by the CRC-trained personnel, who were blinded to the study treatments." and "Lean body mass, total and trunk fat mass, and total and trunk percentage fat were assessed ... by dual energy x-ray absorptiometry (Hologic DP-W; Hologic, Bedford, MA, USA)." Comment: The outcome assessor for anthropometric/body composition measurements was explicitly blinded to treatment allocation. The main outcomes here were body weight/BMI and DXA-derived body composition and serum lipids, which are objective measurements and unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although some compliance interviews involved the investigator, the reported outcome measurements of interest were made by blinded personnel and automated/standardized methods, so detection bias is low.

Quote: "Thirty subjects were randomly assigned to one of the following three diets for 21 wk ... Five subjects dropped out from the study before the second visit; four females and one male. The reasons for not continuing in the study were as follows: one had medical reasons and four lost interest. There were no significant differences in the subject characteristics between those who completed and those who did not complete the study (data not shown). Of the 25 subjects who completed the study..." Comment: Attrition was small overall (5/30, about 17%) and the paper reports no meaningful baseline differences between completers and non-completers, with dropout reasons not obviously related to the outcomes. The remaining analyses and tables are based on 25 completers, but given the modest and apparently balanced/neutral attrition, bias from missing outcome data is unlikely.
